title: Roast rack of lamb
subtitle: French-trimmed, herb-crusted, medium-rare
excerpt: Two French-trimmed racks of lamb roasted at high heat for 25 minutes until pink throughout. A quick, impressive centrepiece for a dinner party or Easter lunch.
sourceNotes: Classic French-trimmed rack technique, widely used in British restaurant and home cooking.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Apply the herb crust up to 4 hours ahead and refrigerate uncovered. Bring to room temperature for 30 minutes before cooking.

glossaryTerms.definition: Scraping the meat and connective tissue from the rib bones of a rack of lamb so the bones are clean and white when the rack is served.
Leaving cooked meat to stand uncovered before carving, allowing the juices to redistribute evenly through the muscle fibres rather than running onto the board.

content.text: A rack of lamb is a quick roast: 25 minutes from raw to carved, with the most hands-on time spent applying the herb crust. The internal temperature is everything here. At 58 degrees C the lamb is pink and juicy; at 65 it is cooked through and still acceptable; above that it is a waste of a good rack.
Heat the oven to 220 degrees C fan. In a food processor or by hand, blitz the breadcrumbs, parsley, rosemary, garlic, and olive oil until a coarse, bright green paste forms.
Season the racks with salt and pepper. Brush the meaty side of each rack with Dijon mustard, then press the herb crust firmly on top in an even layer.
Place the racks crust-side up in a roasting tin, with the bones pointing upward. The
bones protect the meat and allow air to circulate. Roast for 22 to 25 minutes for medium-rare (internal temperature 58 to 60 degrees C).
Transfer to a warm board and
for 10 minutes. Carve between the ribs into individual cutlets to serve.

items.symptom: The crust is falling off during carving
items.cause: The mustard was applied to a warm surface so it did not set, or the crust was too dry.
items.fix: Apply the mustard to a completely cold rack. The crust adheres best to cold meat. Press it on firmly with your hands.

items.symptom: The crust is golden but the meat is overcooked
items.cause: The oven was too hot or the racks were small.
items.fix: Use a thermometer to check doneness. A thermapen reads instantly. Aim for 58 degrees C in the thickest part of the meat, not the bone.

items.symptom: The crust is pale and soft after roasting
items.cause: The oven was not properly preheated, or the racks were refrigerator-cold when they went in.
items.fix: Always allow the meat to come to room temperature for 30 minutes. A well-preheated oven at 220 degrees C should colour the crust in the first 10 minutes.

items.symptom: One rack is done before the other
items.cause: Uneven rack sizes, or one was positioned differently in the oven.
items.fix: Lean the racks against each other in the tin so the bones interlock. This keeps them upright and allows them to cook more evenly.

content.text: Replace the herb crust with a pistachio and mint version: blitz shelled pistachios, fresh mint, and olive oil instead of breadcrumbs and rosemary. For a simpler finish, skip the crust entirely and just season well with salt and a rub of rosemary oil.

Rack of lamb is a French technique adopted into British fine dining and then into domestic cooking by the 1980s. The French trimming of the bones is purely aesthetic, but has become the expected presentation. It is the standard Easter centrepiece in households that want something more elegant than a leg, and faster than a shoulder.
